cases.prompt: write a README for this project
my readme is a mess, restructure it
can u make the readme less bloated
draft a professional README for my CLI tool
improve the readme so people actually try the project
review my README and tell me what's missing
the readme reads like AI slop, tighten it up
add a quick start section to the readme
new OSS project, I need a landing-page quality readme
readme.md for a python library pls
the readme is out of date after the refactor, sync it with the code
our README still documents the old CLI flags, bring it up to date
make my readme look as polished as the top github projects
add topics to my repo so people can find it
set a social preview image for the repo
write CONTRIBUTING.md for this project
add a bug report template
protect the main branch
audit my repo, how professional is it overall?
write API reference docs for this module
generate a changelog from the commit history
make my github profile readme with stats widgets
Write a README for this repo - it's a Python CLI that syncs dotfiles across machines
expected_behavior: inspects the repo first and uses real install/run commands, never invented ones
produces H1 + one-line value proposition, at most 5 trust badges, and a copy-pasteable install + smallest-success usage block near the top
orders sections identity -> proof -> activation -> expansion -> participation -> governance and links out for depth instead of inlining it
total length appropriate to the project (roughly 200-800 words for a small CLI)
runs scripts/readme_lint.py on the result and fixes anything it reports

cases.prompt: My README is 900 lines long. Restructure it without losing information.
expected_behavior: moves deep content into docs/ (or linked files) rather than deleting it
front-loads what/why/quick-start into the first screen
adds a table of contents only if the result still exceeds ~400 lines
does not invent new claims or features while restructuring

cases.prompt: Write a readme for a brand-new empty repo - we only have the idea: a rust crate for fuzzy time parsing
expected_behavior: derives a plausible one-line value proposition from the idea and marks unknown details as clearly-labelled placeholders
does not add badges pointing at CI or registries that do not exist yet
keeps the skeleton short and flags which sections to fill in after code exists

cases.prompt: we renamed the env vars and added a new subcommand last sprint - update the README
expected_behavior: diffs the recent changes first and starts from the mapped sections (Configuration for env vars, Usage for the subcommand), then searches the whole README for the old identifiers
preserves the README's existing tone, structure, and formatting - a sync, not a rewrite
prunes old env var names everywhere; if something is deprecated-but-working it is marked with its replacement instead of deleted
verifies commands safely (existence/static checks, dry-runs only - nothing mutating or networked) and lists anything left unverified
re-runs the lint script after the sync

cases.prompt: add badges to my readme
expected_behavior: adds at most ~5 meaningful trust badges (CI status, license, version/coverage where real)
verifies the CI workflow file exists before adding its badge and uses the correct actions badge URL
uses a static shields.io badge for the license and avoids decorative badge walls

cases.prompt: our CLI has no demo, add a terminal gif to the readme
expected_behavior: creates the demo rather than leaving a placeholder: uses the tape-demo skill when installed, otherwise writes a VHS .tape script or recommends installing paldom/terminaltor
keeps the GIF under ~5 MB, commits it to a repo path (not an external host), and gives it meaningful alt text
places the demo above Installation per the CLI template
shows real commands from this repo in the demo, never invented ones

cases.prompt: the top of our readme looks amateur, give it a proper hero header
expected_behavior: builds a centered hero stack (logo/tagline/nav row/badges) using the copy-adapt blocks from the templates reference, or a plain H1 route if the project has no logo
styles badges as one designed element (single shields style and color family), never a badge wall
nav-row links point only at sections or sites that actually exist
uses a dark/light picture element for the logo when a dark variant exists
